title: Modern Britain: 20th Century to Today
overview:
The 20th century brought enormous changes to Britain through two World Wars, the loss of empire, and the creation of the welfare state. World War I (1914-1918) was devastating, with millions of British soldiers killed or wounded. Women contributed significantly to the war effort by working in factories and on farms, and in 1918, some women were given the right to vote for the first time. Full voting equality came in 1928 when all women over 21 gained the vote, the same age as men.

World War II (1939-1945) saw Britain stand against Nazi Germany. During the Blitz, German bombers attacked British cities, especially London, but the country did not surrender. Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in 1940 and inspired the nation with his speeches. The war effort again brought social change, with women taking on many roles traditionally done by men. After the war, people wanted a fairer society.

In 1948, the National Health Service (NHS) was founded, providing free healthcare for all British citizens. This was part of the welfare state created by the Labour government after the war, which also included improved education and social security. However, Britain's global power was declining. The British Empire gradually transformed into the Commonwealth, a voluntary association of independent countries, most of which were former British colonies. India gained independence in 1947, marking the beginning of decolonization.

From the 1950s onward, Britain became increasingly multicultural as people from Commonwealth countries, the Caribbean, Africa, and Asia came to live and work in the UK. This immigration enriched British society with diverse cultures, foods, and traditions. Britain joined the European Economic Community (now the European Union) in 1973, though it voted to leave in 2016 and officially left in 2020. In recent decades, devolution has given Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own parliaments or assemblies with powers over local matters. Today, the UK is a modern, multicultural democracy with a constitutional monarchy, playing an important role in international organizations like the United Nations, NATO, and the Commonwealth.
key_facts: World War I lasted from 1914 to 1918 and resulted in millions of casualties.
In 1918, some women were given the right to vote for the first time.
In 1928, all women over 21 gained equal voting rights with men.
World War II lasted from 1939 to 1945, with Britain fighting Nazi Germany.
The Blitz was the German bombing campaign against British cities, especially London.
Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in 1940 and led Britain through WWII.
The National Health Service (NHS) was founded in 1948, providing free healthcare.
The welfare state was created after WWII, including improved education and social security.
India gained independence from Britain in 1947, beginning decolonization.
The British Empire became the Commonwealth, a voluntary association of independent nations.
From the 1950s, people from Commonwealth countries came to Britain, creating a multicultural society.
Britain joined the European Economic Community (later EU) in 1973.
Britain voted to leave the EU in 2016 and officially left in 2020 (Brexit).
Devolution gave Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own parliaments/assemblies.
The UK is a constitutional monarchy and a member of the UN, NATO, and Commonwealth.
Modern Britain is a multicultural democracy with diverse cultures and traditions.
memory_hook: Think: Two World Wars changed everything, the NHS was born in 1948, the Empire became the Commonwealth, and Britain became multicultural – all leading to today's modern, diverse democracy.

questions.prompt: When was the National Health Service (NHS) founded?
questions.explanation: The NHS was founded in 1948, providing free healthcare to all UK citizens at the point of use.

questions.prompt: Who was the British Prime Minister during most of World War II?
questions.explanation: Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in 1940 and led Britain through World War II with his inspiring speeches and leadership.

questions.prompt: When did women gain equal voting rights with men in Britain?
questions.explanation: In 1928, all women over 21 gained the right to vote, achieving equal voting rights with men. Some women over 30 had gained the vote in 1918.

questions.prompt: Which country's independence in 1947 marked the beginning of decolonization?
questions.explanation: India gained independence from Britain in 1947, marking the beginning of the end of the British Empire and the start of decolonization.
The Blitz was the German bombing campaign against British cities during WWII, particularly targeting London and other major cities.

questions.prompt: When did Britain join the European Economic Community (later EU)?
questions.explanation: Britain joined the European Economic Community (which later became the European Union) in 1973.

questions.prompt: What did the British Empire transform into after WWII?
questions.explanation: The British Empire gradually transformed into the Commonwealth, a voluntary association of independent countries, most of which were former British colonies.
Devolution means giving Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own parliaments or assemblies with powers to make decisions on local matters.

questions.prompt: When did Britain officially leave the European Union?
questions.explanation: Britain voted to leave the EU in a 2016 referendum and officially left in 2020, an event commonly known as Brexit.

flashcards: When did some women get the vote?
When did all women get equal voting rights?
Who was PM during most of WWII?
When did Britain join the EEC?
When did Britain leave the EU?
